title: Choosing the Right Emergency Shelter Type
summary: Three main shelter types — tarp, natural lean-to, and debris hut — have very different trade-offs in speed, warmth, and required materials. Choosing the wrong type wastes critical time or leaves you cold overnight.

title: Build a Debris Hut for Overnight Warmth
summary: Build a debris hut — a compact, fully enclosed natural shelter — that uses body heat and thick leaf insulation to stay warm down to 0°F without fire. It takes several hours to build but is the most thermally effective no-equipment shelter in forested environments.

title: Build a Natural Lean-To Frame Shelter
summary: Build a wind-and-rain shelter using branches and natural materials — no tarp needed. A lean-to can be constructed in under an hour and provides immediate overhead cover, but requires a fire on its open side for cold-weather warmth.

title: Emergency Food Procurement: Building Skills and Supplies Before Disaster Strikes
summary: When supply chains collapse during extended emergencies, your ability to feed your household depends on decisions made long before the disaster. This guide covers how to build a deep food stockpile, learn active food procurement skills — fishing, foraging, gardening, and food preservation — and prepare for scenarios where stored food alone is not enough.

title: Edible Plant Foraging in an Emergency
summary: A structured walkthrough for identifying and safely consuming wild plants when conventional food is unavailable. Plant misidentification is a potentially fatal risk — this guide covers the identification process, three high-confidence starter edibles, toxic lookalike warnings, and what to do if poisoning is suspected.

title: Primitive Fishing in a Survival Situation
summary: When stranded without fishing gear for multiple days, improvised hooks, passive fish traps, and fish weirs can secure critical protein and calories. Passive methods (weirs, traps) are preferred because they work unsupervised while you handle other survival priorities.

title: Signal for Rescue: Be Seen and Be Heard
summary: When lost or stranded on land, systematic signaling — using the Rule of Three, a whistle, mirror, and ground symbols — dramatically improves your chances of being found. Staying put and signaling from a visible location is almost always more effective than trying to self-rescue.

title: Stay Put vs Move When Lost on Land
summary: If you are truly lost on land but not in immediate danger, staying near your last known location is almost always safer and makes you far easier to find. Move only to escape an active hazard or reach clearly safer ground nearby.

title: Food Safety After Power Loss: What to Keep and What to Toss
summary: When power goes out, two rules govern food safety: keep the doors closed as long as possible to preserve cold, and never taste food to judge whether it's safe. This card gives you the exact time windows and decision rules for refrigerator, freezer, and cooler scenarios.

title: Wildfire Preparedness: Go Bag, Evacuation Plan, and Defensible Space
summary: Surviving a wildfire requires preparation done well before fire season: a ready go-bag, clear evacuation routes, and maintained defensible space around your home are the three pillars of wildfire preparedness. The single most important action is leaving early.

title: Flood Preparedness: Know Your Risk and Plan Your Escape
summary: Flooding is the most common and deadliest natural disaster in the U.S. Preparation done before a flood — knowing your zone, planning your escape routes, and building an emergency kit — can be the difference between safety and tragedy.

title: Tornado Preparedness: Safe Place, Kit, and Family Plan
summary: Surviving a tornado depends almost entirely on preparation done before the storm. Identify your safe shelter now, build an emergency kit, and make sure every household member knows exactly where to go when a tornado warning is issued.

title: CPR: How It Works and When to Use It
summary: CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) manually circulates blood to the brain and vital organs when the heart has stopped. Bystander CPR can double or triple survival odds — but fewer than half of all cardiac arrests in the US receive it. Understanding how CPR works, how it differs for adults, children, and infants, and when to use an AED helps you act confidently when seconds count.

title: Sprains and Suspected Fractures
summary: Most limb injuries after a fall, twist, or impact look the same — you cannot reliably tell a sprain from a fracture without an X-ray. This guide walks through how to assess the injury, when to call emergency services, how to immobilize and cool the limb, and when it is safe to wait for an urgent care appointment versus going to an emergency room.

title: Sprain or Suspected Fracture: Immobilize and Cool
summary: If you cannot tell whether an injury is a sprain or fracture, treat it as a fracture. Stop the person from moving the limb, apply an improvised splint in the position found, cool with a wrapped ice pack for 20 minutes, and elevate. Check circulation below the injury before and after splinting.

title: Cardiac Arrest: Start CPR Immediately
summary: If someone is unresponsive and not breathing normally, they are likely in cardiac arrest. Start CPR immediately — every minute without compressions reduces survival odds by 7–10%. You do not need formal training to help: push hard and fast in the center of the chest until emergency services arrive.

title: Preparing for a Nuclear or Radiological Emergency
summary: Nuclear and radiological emergencies require specific advance preparation beyond a standard emergency kit. Identifying your best shelter room, stocking enough food and water, keeping a hand-crank radio, and understanding shelter-in-place and decontamination basics are the core pillars. Potassium iodide (KI) plays a limited, specific supporting role — it protects only the thyroid from radioactive iodine and should only be used if directed by authorities.

title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis Warning Signs
summary: Anaphylaxis is a severe, rapidly progressing allergic reaction that can cause airway, breathing, or circulation failure within minutes. Epinephrine is the only effective first-line treatment — antihistamines alone cannot reverse anaphylaxis.

title: Choking: Unresponsive Adult or Child
summary: When a choking person becomes unresponsive, CPR with chest compressions becomes the primary intervention — each compression may help dislodge the obstruction while also circulating blood. Look for the object in the mouth only if it is clearly visible.

title: Choking: Responsive Adult or Child
summary: Choking is a life-threatening emergency when a person cannot breathe, speak, or cough effectively due to an object blocking the airway. Immediate back blows and abdominal thrusts can dislodge the object and restore breathing in minutes.

title: Bleeding Control: Direct Pressure First
summary: Direct pressure is the first and most important action for controlling serious external bleeding. Severe blood loss can cause death within minutes — apply firm, uninterrupted pressure immediately and call emergency services.

title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help
summary: Hypothermia occurs when the body's core temperature drops dangerously low from cold or wet exposure. Gentle rewarming of the core is critical — rough handling or rapid external rewarming of the limbs can trigger fatal cardiac arrest in moderate to severe cases.

title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help
summary: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency in which the body can no longer regulate its temperature. Permanent organ damage or death can occur within minutes — immediate cooling and emergency services are both required.

title: Send an SOS Signal Using Morse Code
summary: How to send the internationally recognized SOS distress signal (· · · − − − · · ·) using a flashlight, whistle, tapping, blinking, or mirror — step by step, regardless of what tools you have available.

title: Morse Code Alphabet and Numbers
summary: The complete International Morse Code character table (A–Z and 0–9) as standardized by ITU-R M.1677-1, with timing rules and tips for using Morse code to signal in an emergency.

title: EMP (Electromagnetic Pulse): What Stops Working and What to Do
summary: A high-altitude nuclear detonation can produce an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) that disables or damages unprotected electronics and grid infrastructure across a large area — potentially a region or more. EMP from a nuclear event means the grid failure is part of a broader nuclear emergency, not a standalone power outage.

title: Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS): Signs, Stages, and Care
summary: Acute radiation syndrome (ARS) occurs when the body receives a high dose of penetrating radiation in a short time. The deceptive latent phase — where a person feels well for hours to weeks — is the most dangerous misconception. Anyone who may have received a significant radiation dose should seek medical evaluation even if they feel fine.

title: Understanding Nuclear and Radiological Threats
summary: Nuclear weapons, dirty bombs, and nuclear power plant accidents are distinct threats with different effects, different scales of damage, and different protective actions. This reference explains blast zones, fallout patterns, radiation dose concepts, and what protection factor means — so you understand why sheltering actions work.

title: Dirty Bomb (Radiological Dispersal Device): Immediate Response
summary: A dirty bomb uses conventional explosives to disperse radioactive material — it is NOT a nuclear weapon and does not produce a nuclear blast or mushroom cloud. The explosion itself is the immediate danger. After the blast, moving away from the site and decontaminating are the priorities.

title: Preparing for a Nuclear or Radiological Emergency
summary: Nuclear and radiological emergencies require specific advance preparation beyond a standard emergency kit. Knowing your best shelter room, having the right supplies for a 24-hour shelter-in-place, and understanding potassium iodide can make a critical difference if an event occurs.

title: Radiation Decontamination: Remove Fallout from Your Body
summary: If you were outdoors during or after a nuclear detonation or radiological release, removing your outer clothing and washing exposed skin can eliminate up to 90% of radioactive contamination. Act quickly — early decontamination dramatically reduces total radiation dose.

title: Nuclear Fallout: Shelter-in-Place for 24 Hours
summary: Once inside a building after a nuclear detonation, staying sheltered for at least 24 hours is the most important survival action. Radioactive fallout is most dangerous in the first hours and declines rapidly — but only if you stay inside and minimize outside air.

title: Nuclear Explosion: Immediate Response
summary: A nuclear detonation requires fast, decisive action in the first minutes. Getting inside a solid building immediately and staying inside for 24 hours dramatically reduces radiation exposure from fallout — the most survivable threat for people outside the immediate blast zone.

title: Purify Water with a Portable Filter
summary: Portable hollow fiber membrane filters (LifeStraw, Sawyer Squeeze) remove bacteria and protozoa from water without heat or chemicals. They do not remove viruses. In the US and Canada backcountry, the viral risk from natural water sources is generally low — but in flood water, international travel, or areas with sewage contamination, follow filtration with chemical treatment to cover viruses.

title: Purify Water with Chemical Tablets
summary: Chemical purification tablets — especially chlorine dioxide — can disinfect water when boiling is not possible. Chlorine dioxide kills the broadest range of pathogens including Cryptosporidium. Iodine tablets work for bacteria and viruses but not Cryptosporidium, and must not be used by pregnant people or those with thyroid conditions.

title: Disinfect Water with Bleach
summary: Use plain unscented household bleach of known strength to disinfect relatively clear water, then wait at least 30 minutes before drinking. This is a backup method for when boiling is not possible.

title: Purify Water by Boiling
summary: Boiling is the most reliable way to kill bacteria, viruses, and parasites in water during an emergency. Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least 1 minute — or 3 minutes above 5,000 feet elevation — then cool and store it covered.

title: During a Landslide
summary: Immediate actions to take when a landslide is actively occurring or imminent, whether you are indoors, outdoors, or in a vehicle.

title: Balancing Activity and Rest to Avoid Exhaustion
summary: Pace yourself; overexertion increases risk of injury, poor decisions, and hypothermia or heat illness.

title: When to Drop Tasks and Focus on Signaling
summary: When rescuers are likely searching for you or you see or hear them, stop extra tasks and focus on being seen and heard.

title: Signs You Must Switch to Shelter and Warmth
summary: If you are getting cold, wet, or exhausted, stop non-essential tasks and put your energy into staying warm, dry, and out of the wind.

title: Daypack-Size Emergency Kit for Hikes
summary: Add core essentials to your hiking pack: navigation, light, extra clothing, shelter, water, food, first aid, fire, tools, and signaling.

title: Pocket Always-With-You Kit
summary: Carry a tiny set of essentials—whistle, light, basic bandages, fire starter, and emergency blanket—on your person, not just in your pack.

title: When Not to Use a Knife
summary: Skip the knife if you are cold, exhausted, unstable, or tempted to use it for risky tasks like prying, throwing, or self-defense.

title: Safe Cutting Tasks for Shelter and Repairs
summary: Use knives for small, controlled tasks—cutting cord, trimming branches, making stakes—not for heavy chopping or prying.

title: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
summary: Keep your body and others out of your knife’s path, work slowly, and stop using knives when you are cold, exhausted, or impaired.

title: Everyday Cordage Uses in Emergencies
summary: Cordage is most useful for shelter setup, securing gear, simple splints, and improvised repairs—not for climbing or rescue.

title: Simple Loops and Attachment Knots (Figure-Eight on a Bight, Bowline)
summary: Use simple, non-slipping loops to attach lines to objects or create fixed loops for gear; do not use them for life-safety loads unless trained.

title: Managing Small Wounds and Skin Problems
summary: Clean small cuts and blisters early, keep them dry and covered, and watch closely for signs of infection.

title: Human Waste and Washing Basics
summary: Keep waste and washing well away from water sources and camp, and bury human waste only where local rules allow.

title: Hand Hygiene and Food Safety in the Field
summary: Clean your hands before eating and after toilet use, and keep food prep surfaces and utensils as clean and dry as possible.

title: When It’s Better to Risk Untreated Water
summary: If you are at high risk of life-threatening dehydration and have no way to treat water, drinking the best available untreated water may be safer than not drinking at all.

title: Collecting and Storing Water Safely Before Treatment
summary: Use the cleanest container you have, avoid dipping with dirty hands or gear, and protect stored water from re-contamination.

title: Choosing the Safest Available Water Source
summary: Pick the clearest, most upstream, and least contaminated-looking source you can, then plan to treat it.

title: Ground, Fire, and Passive Visual Signals
summary: Create big, simple, high-contrast shapes on the ground and use bright materials or smoke so rescuers can spot you from air or ground.

title: Simple Survival Priorities: What Comes First
summary: For most short-term emergencies, focus on safety, first aid, shelter or warmth, signaling, and then water; food and complex tasks can wait.

title: STOP-Think-Observe-Plan (First 30 Minutes)
summary: Pause, calm down, check for injuries and hazards, then make a simple plan instead of walking blindly.

title: Weather-Related Red Flag Symptoms (Heat, Cold, Air, Lightning)
summary: Confusion, chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, seizures, or loss of consciousness during weather exposure are medical emergencies.

title: Basic Altitude Illness Prevention for Trekkers
summary: Ascend gradually, include rest days, stay hydrated, avoid alcohol and overexertion, and stop or descend if symptoms appear.

title: Everyday Sun Protection and UV Safety
summary: Limit midday sun, cover skin, use high-SPF sunscreen, and protect your eyes to reduce UV damage and heat stress.

title: Immediate Actions if Thunderstorm or Lightning Approaches Outdoors
summary: Get inside a substantial building or hard-topped metal vehicle immediately; if you cannot, move away from high points, isolated trees, and water.

title: Dust, Ash, and Airborne Irritants After Storms, Eruptions, or Collapses
summary: Avoid breathing dust and ash, use appropriate masks if available, stay upwind, and clean up gently with moisture to avoid re-suspending particles.

title: Using AQI and Basic Smoke/Air Quality Precautions
summary: Check AQI and reduce outdoor exertion, close windows, and protect sensitive groups first as air quality worsens.

title: Cold-Wet Exposure: Rain, Wind, and Chill
summary: In cold, wet, windy conditions, staying dry and sheltered is as important as temperature; seek windbreaks, change wet clothes, and insulate from the ground.

title: Staying Warm and Dry in Cold Weather (Prevention)
summary: Dress in layers, stay dry, cover extremities, limit wind and wet exposure, and avoid overexertion that makes you sweat heavily.

title: Working or Exercising Safely in High Heat
summary: Increase water intake, schedule harder work for cooler times, take frequent shade breaks, and build up activity gradually in hot conditions.

title: Everyday Extreme Heat Precautions (Non-Emergency)
summary: Stay cool, hydrated, out of direct sun, and check on high-risk people during extreme heat.

title: Child and Pet Safety Around Emergency Lighting
summary: Keep all flames and hot lights out of reach of children and pets, and teach kids to use flashlights safely instead of matches or candles.

title: Fuel-Burning Lanterns and Heaters: Last-Resort Guidance
summary: Avoid fuel-burning lanterns and heaters indoors; if you must use one, follow manufacturer instructions strictly, keep ventilation adequate, and never sleep with it running.

title: Using Flashlights and Battery Lanterns Safely
summary: Use battery-powered lights on stable surfaces, away from water and heat, and keep spare batteries nearby; they are the safest lighting option in outages.

title: When to Leave and Not Return Until Professionally Inspected
summary: If a building has major structural, electrical, or gas damage, leave and do not return until authorities or qualified inspectors say it is safe.

title: Safe Behavior Inside a Damaged Building
summary: If you must go inside a lightly damaged building, move slowly, avoid hazards, and be ready to leave immediately if anything seems unsafe.

title: Checking if a Building Is Safe to Enter After a Disaster
summary: Do an external safety check first: if you see major structural damage, leaning walls, sagging roof, gas smell, or downed lines, do not enter.

title: When Not to Touch Utility Controls and Call the Utility Instead
summary: If there is flooding, visible damage, fire, or gas smell near utility controls, do not touch them; evacuate and call the utility or emergency services instead.

title: Recognizing and Responding to a Gas Leak
summary: If you smell gas or suspect a leak, leave the building immediately without using switches or phones inside, then call the gas company or emergency services from a safe location.

title: Preventing CO Poisoning During Outages
summary: Never use fuel-burning devices indoors or in attached structures; keep generators, grills, and vehicles outside and far from doors, windows, and vents.

title: Carbon Monoxide Basics and Symptoms
summary: Carbon monoxide is an odorless, colorless gas from fuel-burning devices that can kill quickly; early symptoms include headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, and confusion.

title: Apartment and Multi-Unit Housing Generator Safety
summary: Portable generators are usually unsafe and often prohibited for balconies, hallways, stairwells, or shared indoor spaces; use non-fuel alternatives or official building plans instead.

title: Powering Appliances Safely with a Generator
summary: Plug only essential appliances directly into the generator with proper cords; never connect a portable generator to home wiring unless a qualified electrician installed a transfer switch.

title: Safe Placement and Operation of Portable Generators
summary: Always use generators outdoors at least 6 m / 20 ft from doors, windows, and vents, with exhaust pointing away; never run them indoors or in garages.

title: Preparing Your Kitchen for Future Outages
summary: Equip your kitchen with thermometers, ice, coolers, and shelf-stable foods so you can keep food safe during outages.

guide_parent_topics.name: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages

title: Keeping Food Safe During a Power Outage
summary: Keep fridge and freezer doors closed; a refrigerator keeps food safe about 4 hours, and a full freezer 24–48 hours, if doors stay shut.

guide_parent_topics.name: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages

title: Safe Use of Extension Cords and Avoiding Overloads
summary: Use heavy-duty, undamaged cords rated for the load, plug them directly into outlets or generator sockets, and avoid daisy-chaining or overloading power strips.

title: Charging Plans and Backup Power Options
summary: Plan several safe ways to charge essential devices: mains power when available, power banks, vehicle charging, and if needed, generators or solar chargers used safely.

title: Phone Battery Conservation in Emergencies
summary: Turn your phone into a low-power emergency tool: dim the screen, enable battery saver, close power-hungry apps, and use it mainly for key calls and messages.

title: Safe Home Lighting During Blackouts
summary: Use flashlights and battery-powered lanterns for light; avoid candles whenever possible.

title: Deciding Whether to Stay or Go During a Blackout
summary: Stay if the building is sound and you can manage temperature and medical needs safely; leave if heat, cold, damage, or health risks make staying unsafe.

title: First 30 Minutes of a Power Outage
summary: Stay calm, check for hazards, protect appliances, keep cold food closed up, and switch to safe battery lighting while you confirm whether the outage is local or widespread.

title: When to Seek Public Shelters or Warming Centers
summary: If you cannot keep your home above a safe temperature or must consider unsafe heating methods, go to a public shelter or warming center if travel is safe.

title: Prioritizing Tasks in the First 30 Minutes of Exposure
summary: Use your first 30 minutes to get out of wind and rain, adjust clothing, protect hands and feet, and secure shelter; leave advanced fire tasks for later.

title: Deciding When Shelter Matters More Than Fire
summary: In most cold emergencies, getting under shelter and into dry, insulated clothing is more reliable and safer than trying to build a fire.

title: Recognizing and Responding to Carbon Monoxide Exposure
summary: Treat any CO alarm as an emergency: get everyone into fresh air immediately, then call emergency services from a safe location.

title: Unsafe Indoor Heating Methods to Avoid
summary: Never use generators, charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens to heat your home; they can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning and fires.

title: Safe Options for Heating Indoors During Power Outages
summary: Use built-in heating systems, properly vented fireplaces or wood stoves, and listed portable electric heaters; avoid unvented fuel devices.

title: Fuel Storage and Handling During Emergencies
summary: Store fuels outside living areas in approved containers, away from heat sources, and never use them indoors for open burning.

title: Safe Use of Portable Heaters Indoors
summary: Keep space heaters three feet from anything that can burn, plug them directly into wall outlets, and turn them off when you sleep or leave the room.

title: Fully Extinguishing Outdoor Fires
summary: Drown the fire with water, stir the ashes, and repeat until everything is cold to the touch; never leave warm or smoking material behind.

title: When Not to Attempt a Fire in Wet Conditions
summary: Skip building a fire if it is unsafe, banned, or would distract from higher-priority tasks like shelter and getting dry.

title: Building a Fire Lay That Survives Rain and Snow
summary: Raise the fire off soaked ground, protect it from above, and stack fuel so it can dry and catch as the fire grows.

title: Finding and Using Dry Tinder in Wet Weather
summary: Look in sheltered places for the driest fine material, and use more tinder than usual to overcome dampness.

title: Choosing and Preparing Dry Fuel
summary: Collect small, dead, and dry wood from off the ground and sheltered areas, and split larger pieces to expose drier inner wood if you have the skill and tools.

title: Safe Fire Site Selection and Wind Management
summary: Choose an open, clear area away from vegetation, roots, and overhead branches, and account for wind so sparks and smoke blow away from people and shelter.

title: Overnight Survival in a Vehicle in Cold Weather
summary: Stay in the vehicle, insulate yourself, run the engine only periodically with a clear exhaust pipe, and avoid sleeping with continuous engine or heater use.

title: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet
summary: Get under cover, remove wet layers if possible, insulate yourself from the ground, and warm your core with dry clothes or shared insulation.

title: Managing Moisture Inside Tents and Improvised Shelters
summary: Ventilate enough to reduce condensation while keeping wind and rain out, and keep sleeping areas off wet ground.

title: Drying Wet Clothing and Bedding Safely Indoors
summary: Hang wet items spread out in the warmest, best-ventilated space you have, away from heaters or flames that could ignite them.

title: Keeping Dry in Rain and Snow Outdoors
summary: Use water-resistant outer layers, protect hands and feet, and minimize time in heavy rain or slush to prevent dangerous chilling.

title: Keeping Children, Elderly, and Frail Adults Warm Safely
summary: Keep vulnerable people in the warmest room, use safe layers and blankets, check them often, and avoid unsafe heating methods.

title: Layering Clothing and Bedding for Maximum Warmth
summary: Use several loose, dry layers and insulate underneath as well as on top to retain heat.

title: Staying Warm in a Cold Home Without Power
summary: Gather everyone in one small room, block drafts, insulate floors and windows, and use safe blankets and clothing—not fuel devices—to stay warm.

title: Using Buildings and Tarps for Temporary Roof and Wall Protection
summary: Use heavy-duty tarps to cover damaged roofs or broken windows, but stay off unsafe structures.

title: Low A-Frame Tarp Shelter for Cold Nights
summary: Pitch the tarp low in an A-frame with both sides close to the ground to conserve heat and block wind.

title: Fast Tarp Lean-To for Wind and Rain
summary: Pitch a tarp as a low lean-to facing away from the wind, with thick insulation underneath you.

title: Simple No-Tools Emergency Shelter Inside Buildings
summary: Close off one small interior room, block drafts, and build a warm “nest” with layers above and below you.

title: Choosing a Safe Spot for Emergency Shelter
summary: Pick the safest dry, stable shelter you can reach, away from flood, fire, wind, and falling-debris hazards.

title: Alert Systems: Smoke Alarms, Weather Radio, and Phone Alerts
summary: Use layered alerts: working smoke alarms, a weather radio, and emergency phone alerts so one failure does not leave you without warning.

title: Basic Home Hazard and Injury Prevention Check
summary: Walk through your home to reduce the everyday fire, trip, electrical, and chemical hazards that become worse during disasters.

title: Utility Shutoff and Gas Leak Safety
summary: Know where your main shutoffs are and when to use them, but only shut utilities off if authorities recommend it or if there is an immediate hazard and you have been shown how.

title: Home Fire Safety and Escape Readiness
summary: Install and test smoke alarms, keep extinguishers accessible, and practice two escape routes from every room with a family meeting point.

title: Pet Evacuation and Shelter Planning
summary: Know where your pets can go before you need to evacuate. Confirm shelters, hotels, friends, or boarding options ahead of time.

title: Pet Emergency Go-Kit
summary: Prepare a pet kit with food, water, medications, records, and containment so your animal can evacuate or shelter with you.

title: Power-Dependent Medical Devices Planning
summary: If someone depends on powered medical equipment, plan now for outages, backup batteries, alternate locations, and emergency contacts.

title: Household Critical Documents Kit
summary: Gather copies of IDs, insurance, legal, financial, property, and emergency documents into a protected grab-and-go kit before disaster damages the originals.

title: Medical Information and Health Document Backup
summary: Keep copies of medical summaries, allergies, insurance information, and provider contacts in both paper and digital form so care can continue when systems are disrupted.

title: Essential Medication Backup Planning
summary: Work with clinicians and pharmacists to maintain a backup supply of essential medicines and a clear medication list before disasters disrupt access.

title: Shelter-in-Place Kit (Home)
summary: Prepare a dedicated kit so your household can remain indoors safely for at least three days during storms, air-quality events, chemical releases, or security incidents.

title: Practice Drills and Plan Refresh
summary: Plans work better when you rehearse them. Run simple drills for evacuation, meeting places, and communication, then update your plan based on what you learn.

title: School, Work, and Caregiver Coordination
summary: Your family plan should fit the emergency procedures used by schools, workplaces, and caregivers—not conflict with them.

title: Meeting Places and Reunion Planning
summary: Choose and document meeting places near home, in the neighborhood, and outside your area so everyone knows where to go if separated.

title: Basic Family Emergency Communication Plan
summary: Write down who to contact, how to reach them, and where to meet if phones, travel, or routines are disrupted during an emergency.

title: Kit Maintenance and Rotation Schedule
summary: Emergency kits only work if you check and refresh them regularly. Replace expired food, water, batteries, and medications on a schedule.

title: Car / Work Mini Emergency Kit (24–72 Hours)
summary: Keep a compact emergency kit in your car or workplace so you can manage delays, sheltering, or being stranded away from home.

title: Personal Evacuation Go-Bag (Adult)
summary: Keep a small bag ready with water, food, light, communications, first aid, hygiene, documents, and basic tools to leave home quickly.

title: 72-Hour Home Survival Kit (Shelter at Home)
summary: Store at least three days of water, food, light, radio, first aid, sanitation, and key documents in one clearly labeled home kit.

title: Cleaning and Disinfection to Prevent Disease Spread
summary: Clean visible dirt first, then disinfect high-touch and contaminated surfaces with an appropriate disinfectant or bleach solution made using safe water.

title: Hand Hygiene When Water or Soap Is Scarce
summary: Wash hands with safe water and soap when possible; otherwise use alcohol-based sanitizer on visibly clean hands and prioritize hygiene before eating, feeding children, or wound care.

title: Emergency Toilet and Waste Management
summary: Keep human waste contained, covered, and away from food, living spaces, and water sources using lined buckets, improvised toilets, or approved disposal methods.

title: Safe Food Handling When Water Is Limited
summary: Use safe water for any food-contact task, and if safe water is scarce, prioritize drinking, medication, hand hygiene before food prep, and rinsing food-contact items.

title: Deciding When to Throw Away Food After an Outage
summary: When power returns, keep perishable foods only if they stayed at or below 4 C / 40 F; when in doubt, throw it out.

title: Freezer Safety During Outages
summary: A full freezer can stay cold about 48 hours, or 24 hours if half-full, if the door stays closed; food with ice crystals may still be safe to refreeze or cook.

title: Refrigerator Safety During Outages
summary: Keep the fridge closed; perishable foods are generally safe for about 4 hours without power if the door stays shut and temperatures stay cold enough.

title: Using Non-Drinking Water Safely
summary: Reserve suspect or lower-quality water for low-risk tasks like flushing or cleaning non-food-contact surfaces, and keep it away from drinking, cooking, wounds, and infant feeding.

title: Emergency Water Storage at Home
summary: Store at least 1 gallon of safe water per person per day for at least 3 days, and ideally up to 2 weeks, in clean food-grade containers.

title: When Treated Water Is Still Unsafe to Drink
summary: Water can remain unsafe after boiling or chlorination if it contains fuel, toxic chemicals, or heavy metals, or if authorities issue a do-not-drink notice.

title: Purifying Water with Household Liquid Bleach
summary: Use plain unscented household bleach of known strength to disinfect relatively clear water, then wait at least 30 minutes before drinking.

title: Choosing Safe Hydration Sources
summary: Use sealed bottled water first; use tap water only if officials say it is safe; otherwise boil or disinfect the clearest available source before drinking.

title: Maritime Electronic Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
summary: At sea, use VHF radio distress calling, DSC, EPIRBs, and other approved electronic systems as primary alerting tools, with visual signals as backup for localization.

title: Maritime Visual Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
summary: On water, use coast-guard-approved visual distress signals such as flares, orange distress flags, smoke, and approved electronic lights; treat land-based signaling patterns only as secondary aids.

title: Maximizing Rescue Visibility (Land)
summary: Combine contrast, motion, light, and sound in one place so you stand out from the landscape to both air and ground searchers.

title: Beacon and Locator Basics (PLB, EPIRB, Satellite Messengers)
summary: Use an approved emergency beacon or SOS-capable satellite messenger as your most reliable long-range distress tool, but still pair it with visual and audible signals.

title: Ground-to-Air Symbols for Aircraft
summary: Use large, high-contrast symbols such as V for assistance and X for medical help so aircraft can understand your situation from above.

title: Mirror and Light Signaling (Land, Day and Night)
summary: Aim sunlight or a bright light at aircraft, boats, or distant people using a mirror or reflective object to create intense flashes.

title: Whistle Distress Signals (Land)
summary: Use three short whistle blasts repeated regularly as your basic distress signal.

title: Stay Put vs Move (Land)
summary: If you are truly lost but not in immediate danger, staying put is usually safer and makes you easier to find; move only to escape danger or reach clearly safer ground.

title: Lost on Land: First 30 Minutes
summary: Stop, calm down, assess your situation, try only a short safe backtrack if the route is obvious, then stay put, shelter, and start signaling.

title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis Warning Signs
summary: Watch for hives plus swelling of the face or throat, trouble breathing, or feeling faint. Treat suspected anaphylaxis as an emergency.

title: Sprains and Suspected Fractures
summary: If you are unsure whether it is a sprain or fracture, treat it as a fracture: immobilize, avoid weight-bearing, use cold packs, and get medical assessment.

title: Choking: Unresponsive Adult or Child
summary: Call for help, start CPR with chest compressions, and check the mouth only for an object you can clearly see and remove easily.

title: Choking: Responsive Adult or Child
summary: If the person cannot cough, speak, or breathe, give back blows and abdominal thrusts (or chest thrusts when appropriate) and call for help.

title: Burns That Need Emergency Care
summary: Large, deep, or critical-area burns, or burns from chemicals, electricity, or inhalation need urgent medical attention.

title: First Aid for Burns (Minor)
summary: Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 20 minutes, remove tight items, and cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing.

title: Dehydration: Signs and Immediate Care
summary: Move to a cooler place and give small frequent sips of safe fluids if the person is awake and not vomiting.

title: Wound Infection: Red Flags
summary: Watch for spreading redness, swelling, warmth, worsening pain, pus, red streaks, or fever, and seek care early if they appear.

title: After Landslide
summary: Stay away from unstable ground and damaged structures until authorities or qualified experts say it is safe.

title: During Landslide
summary: Move quickly out of the slide path toward safer stable ground. Watch for additional slides or flooding.

title: Tornado Safety in a Mobile Home
summary: Leave mobile homes early for a sturdy shelter when tornado warnings are issued. Mobile homes are not safe tornado shelters.

title: Tornado Safety in a Vehicle
summary: If possible, drive to a sturdy shelter immediately. Do not try to shelter under an overpass.

title: Driving in Flood Conditions
summary: Never drive through flooded roads. Turn around, find another route, and move to higher ground if needed.

title: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
summary: Food handling priorities when refrigeration and cooking are limited.

title: Sanitation and Waste Handling Basics
summary: Clean water, hand hygiene, and waste handling basics during disruption.

title: Communication Outage Basics
summary: Fallback communication habits when networks are overloaded or unavailable.

title: Power Outage Basics
summary: Basic lighting, food, refrigeration, charging, and safety actions during blackouts.

title: Night Signaling and Battery Conservation
summary: At night, light signals can help rescuers find you, but battery and warmth conservation still matter.

title: Stay Put vs Move
summary: In many lost-person situations, staying near the last known point can improve rescue chances, but there are exceptions.

title: Signal for Rescue: Stay Visible and Be Heard
summary: If you are lost or stranded, make yourself easier to locate with visible, audible, and light-based signals while protecting yourself from the environment.

title: Shelter in Place Basics
summary: Sometimes staying inside is safer than evacuating. Prepare to remain indoors with supplies and information.

title: Evacuation Go-Bag Checklist
summary: Keep a fast-grab bag ready for evacuations so you are not scrambling during a crisis.

title: Make a Family Emergency Plan
summary: A good emergency plan tells everyone how to reconnect, where to meet, and what to do if communication fails.

title: Build a 72-Hour Emergency Kit
summary: Build a simple grab-and-stay emergency kit with water, food, light, first aid basics, medicines, documents, and communication tools.

title: Emergency Water Storage
summary: Store emergency water before disasters so you are not forced to improvise under pressure.

title: Disinfect Water with Bleach
summary: If you cannot boil water, emergency disinfection with the correct unscented household bleach and dosage may be a backup option.

title: Purify Water by Boiling
summary: Bring clear water to a rolling boil for at least 1 minute, or 3 minutes at high altitude, then cool and store it covered.

title: When a Wound Needs Urgent Medical Care
summary: Some wounds need urgent professional care even if bleeding is partly controlled.

title: Severe Bleeding: Get Help Fast
summary: Severe bleeding is an emergency. Apply direct pressure immediately and get emergency help.

title: Bleeding Control: Direct Pressure First
summary: Press hard directly on the wound, keep constant pressure, and get emergency help for life-threatening bleeding.

title: Minor Cuts and Scrapes: Clean and Cover
summary: Rinse the wound with clean water, remove visible dirt, cover it with a clean dressing, and watch for infection.

title: Cold Weather Prevention and Layering
summary: Stay dry, layer clothing, protect extremities, and limit exposure during cold, wet, or windy conditions.

title: Frostbite: Protect and Rewarm Safely
summary: Protect the area from further cold, avoid walking on frostbitten feet, and rewarm in warm water only if you can prevent refreezing.

title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help
summary: Get the person out of cold and wet conditions, remove wet clothes, warm the body core first, and seek medical care if symptoms are moderate or severe.

title: Heat Safety and Prevention
summary: Hydration, shade, lighter clothing, pacing, and checking vulnerable people reduce the risk of heat illness.

title: Heat Exhaustion: Cool Down and Rehydrate
summary: Move the person to a cooler place, cool them down, and give small sips of fluid; seek care if symptoms worsen or last about an hour.

title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help
summary: Call emergency services immediately, move the person to a cooler place, and start rapid cooling; do not give fluids to a confused or unconscious person.

title: Returning After Wildfire
summary: Return only when authorities say it is safe, and watch for ash, unstable structures, utility damage, and lingering smoke.

title: Wildfire Smoke: Protect Your Air
summary: Stay inside with windows and doors closed, create one cleaner-air room if possible, and avoid adding indoor pollution.

title: Wildfire Evacuation: Leave Early
summary: Leave early when told to evacuate or when conditions are becoming unsafe. Do not wait until roads are blocked or smoke is overwhelming.

title: After the Tornado
summary: After winds stop, watch for debris, injuries, gas leaks, and downed power lines before moving through damaged areas.

title: Tornado Safety: If You Are in a Vehicle or Mobile Home
summary: A sturdy building is safer than a vehicle or mobile home. Leave for shelter if you can do so early and safely.

title: Tornado Safety: Take Shelter Immediately
summary: Get to the lowest interior room of a sturdy building and protect your head and neck immediately.

title: Returning Home After Flooding
summary: Avoid floodwater and damaged structures until hazards are checked. Treat all floodwater as contaminated.

title: Floodwater Safety
summary: Treat all floodwater as contaminated and dangerous. Avoid direct contact whenever possible.

title: Flood Survival: Get to Higher Ground
summary: Move to higher ground immediately and follow evacuation orders. Never walk or drive through floodwater.

title: Earthquake Preparedness Checklist
summary: Prepare before a quake by securing heavy items, practicing drills, and keeping essential supplies and utility knowledge ready.

title: Earthquake Safety: Tsunami Risk After a Quake
summary: If you are near the coast and strong or long shaking occurs, move to higher ground or inland as soon as it is safe.

title: Earthquake Safety: Right After Shaking
summary: Once shaking stops, check for injuries and hazards before moving or re-entering any damaged area.

title: Earthquake Safety: During Shaking
summary: Drop, Cover, and Hold On under sturdy furniture or next to an interior wall away from glass and falling objects.

title: Land Navigation
summary: Use map and compass if you have them; otherwise use only broad terrain awareness for cautious short decisions, and avoid ambitious travel when lost.

title: Tornado Safety
summary: Protective actions during tornadoes.

title: Flood Survival
summary: Safety during flooding events.

title: Earthquake Safety
summary: How to survive and respond to earthquakes.

title: Wound Care
summary: Cleaning, treating, and dressing injuries.

title: Hypothermia
summary: Recognizing and treating dangerous cold exposure.

title: First Aid
summary: Emergency medical treatment before professional help.

title: Dehydration
summary: Prevention and treatment of fluid loss.

title: CPR
summary: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation technique reference.

title: Trapping
summary: Setting traps for small game.

title: Foraging
summary: Identifying edible plants and wild food sources.

title: Food Preservation
summary: Methods to store food long-term.

title: Fishing Techniques
summary: Catching fish for survival food.

title: Water Purification
summary: Methods to make water safer for drinking.

title: Solar Still
summary: Collecting water using solar energy.

title: Shelter Building
summary: Get off wet ground, reduce wind exposure, and create simple overhead cover with whatever materials you have.

title: Knot Tying
summary: Use adjustable knots that can tension and re-tension guy lines to keep tarps and bivouacs stable.

title: Fire Making
summary: Prepare a safe fire site, build a small structure of tinder, kindling, and fuel, then light the tinder and add wood gradually.

title: Morse Code
summary: Communication using dots and dashes for basic signaling.

title: Distress Signaling
summary: Methods to signal for rescue and help.
